relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Heaven’s reign is pictured as lamps waiting for the bridegroom.
Wisdom and folly are shown through readiness.
The foolish carry lamps without sustaining oil.
The wise carry hidden reserves.
Delay tests preparation.
At midnight the cry arrives.
All rise and trim their lamps.
Lack of oil becomes visible too late.
Readiness cannot simply be borrowed.
Those ready enter the feast, and the door closes.
Late calling meets a closed door.
The answer is not knowing.
Watchfulness is required because timing is unknown.
The next parable entrusts possessions to servants.
Each receives according to ability.
Faithfulness works with what is given.
The two-talent servant also multiplies.
Fear hides the entrusted gift.
The lord returns for account.
The five become ten.
Faithful use of little opens joy and greater trust.
The two become four.
The same joy receives the faithful servant.
The one-talent servant reveals his fear-image of the lord.
Fear buries what was entrusted.
The lord exposes the servant’s failure to act.
Even minimal faithful action would have mattered.
The unused gift is transferred.
Capacity grows through use and shrinks through buried refusal.
The warning imagery marks wasted entrustment.
The Son of Humanity comes in glory.
Nations are gathered and separated like sheep and goats.
Right and left reveal different responses to need.
The blessed inherit the prepared reign.
The King was met in hunger, thirst, and strangerhood.
He was met in nakedness, sickness, and prison.
The righteous did not realize they were serving him.
They ask when they saw him as stranger or unclothed.
They ask when they saw him sick or imprisoned.
What is done for the least is done for the King.
Refusal of mercy enters the warning of age-long fire.
Hunger and thirst were ignored.
Stranger, naked, sick, and imprisoned were not visited.
They too ask when they failed to serve him.
What is not done for the least is not done for him.
The chapter closes with age-long correction and age-long life.
